threats.title: Token1 Premium Sign Inversion in computeNAV Distorts Portfolio Valuation
threats.hypothesis_description: In computeNAV, the token1 premium exposure calculation uses longPremium.leftSlot() - shortPremium.leftSlot(), which is the inverse of the token0 convention (shortPremium.rightSlot() - longPremium.rightSlot()). For a vault that is net short and earning token1 premiums, the NAV is understated. For a vault that is net long and owing token1 premiums, the NAV is overstated. This directly affects all deposit/withdrawal fulfillment share pricing via fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetric sign treatment between token0 and token1 premiums is a clear code defect. Short premium represents fees earned and long premium represents fees paid. Net premium should be short-long for both tokens, but token1 computes long-short. This inverts the premium contribution to NAV by 2x the net token1 premium value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: poolExposure0 = short.rightSlot() - long.rightSlot() but poolExposure1 = long.leftSlot() - short.leftSlot(); asymmetric sign convention

preconditions: Vault has at least one open position with non-zero accumulated token1 premiums
Manager calls fulfillDeposits or fulfillWithdrawals using computeNAV

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Asymmetric premium sign convention between token0 and token1
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: poolExposure1 = int256(uint256(longPremium.leftSlot())) - int256(uint256(shortPremium.leftSlot())) inverts the sign relative to poolExposure0
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No consistency check between token0 and token1 premium sign conventions

analysis_questions: Confirm Panoptic's getAccumulatedFeesAndPositionsData return value semantics for short vs long premium
Quantify the NAV distortion for typical vault positions with token1 premiums
Does the distortion systematically favor depositors or withdrawers?
validation_requirements: Create test vault with known short positions earning token1 premium
Compare computed NAV with correct calculation using short-long for token1
Verify the 2x delta in NAV when net premium is non-zero

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A vault earns 1000 USDC in token1 premiums via short positions. NAV should include +1000 but instead includes -1000, understating NAV by 2000. During deposit fulfillment, depositors receive excess shares (totalAssets appears smaller, so more shares per asset). Existing shareholders are diluted. The manager doesn't need to be malicious—this happens automatically.
prerequisites: Non-zero token1 premium accumulation in any pool position

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Systematic mispricing of all deposit and withdrawal fulfillments proportional to token1 premium magnitude
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors and withdrawers; existing shareholders diluted when NAV understated
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot be corrected retroactively; all past fulfillments used incorrect NAV

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test comparing premium sign conventions between token0 and token1

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing NAV output with independent calculation

threats.title: Per-Pool NAV Clamping to Zero Overstates Aggregate NAV by Ignoring Underwater Pool Debt
threats.hypothesis_description: computeNAV applies Math.max(poolExposure0 + poolExposure1, 0) per pool before summing into total NAV. This means negative exposure (real debt) in underwater pools is silently zeroed out while profitable pools contribute their full positive value. If Pool A has +100 exposure and Pool B has -80 exposure, NAV = 100 instead of the correct 20. This inflates NAV, distorting share pricing for all fulfillments.
threats.investigation_rationale: Per-pool clamping is architecturally incorrect for a multi-pool portfolio. Real debt obligations in one pool should offset gains in another for accurate net asset valuation. The clamping creates a permanent positive bias proportional to the magnitude of underwater positions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: nav += uint256(Math.max(poolExposure0 + poolExposure1, 0)); per-pool clamping instead of aggregate

preconditions: Vault has positions in at least two pools
At least one pool has negative net exposure (underwater position)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-pool Math.max(0) clamping before aggregation into nav
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Negative pool exposures are zeroed instead of reducing total NAV
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No aggregate-level clamping after summing all pools

analysis_questions: What is the typical magnitude of negative exposure in Panoptic pools?
Can a vault realistically have pools with significantly different exposure signs?
Does the manager have incentive to exploit this by structuring asymmetric positions?
validation_requirements: Create test with two pools where one is significantly underwater
Compare per-pool clamped NAV with aggregate NAV calculation
Quantify the inflation impact on share pricing

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault has Pool A with +500 underlying exposure and Pool B with -400 underlying exposure. True NAV contribution = 100. Computed NAV = max(500,0) + max(-400,0) = 500. During withdrawal fulfillment, withdrawers receive assets based on inflated NAV, draining 5x more value than justified. Remaining shareholders absorb the eventual Pool B debt settlement.
prerequisites: Multi-pool vault with divergent pool performances

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: NAV overstated by the absolute value of all negative pool exposures; withdrawal fulfillments drain excess assets
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All shareholders; withdrawers extract excess, remaining holders absorb losses
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Losses are irreversible once assets are distributed based on inflated NAV

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing multi-pool NAV test with underwater positions

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: N/A - occurs naturally under market conditions
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Continuous whenever pools have mixed positive/negative exposures
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by independent NAV calculation comparing per-pool vs aggregate clamping

threats.title: Deposit Rollover Rounding Creates Cumulative Epoch-Level Accounting Discrepancy
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeDeposit, unfulfilled amounts per user are computed as queuedDepositAmount - mulDiv(queuedDepositAmount, assetsFulfilled, assetsDeposited). Due to rounding down in mulDiv, each user's remainder is rounded up. The sum of per-user remainders can exceed the epoch-level assetsRemaining set in fulfillDeposits. This creates a growing discrepancy where sum(queuedDeposit[*][epoch+1]) > depositEpochState[epoch+1].assetsDeposited, causing the last users executing deposits in subsequent epochs to receive fewer shares than entitled.
threats.investigation_rationale: The epoch-level rollover (fulfillDeposits) and per-user rollover (executeDeposit) use independent rounding, creating a systematic divergence. With N users, the discrepancy is up to N-1 units per epoch, compounding across epochs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: per-user rollover queuedDeposit[user][epoch+1] += uint128(assetsRemaining) not synchronized with epoch-level depositEpochState[epoch+1].assetsDeposited

preconditions: Partial fulfillment of a deposit epoch with multiple users
Multiple users execute deposits for the same partially-fulfilled epoch

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-user remainder = queuedDepositAmount - userAssetsDeposited does not update epoch-level assetsDeposited
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: sum of per-user rollovers exceeds epoch-level rollover due to rounding
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to reconcile per-user and epoch-level totals after execution

analysis_questions: How large can the discrepancy grow per epoch with realistic user counts?
Does the discrepancy compound across multiple partial fulfillment epochs?
Can a user intentionally split deposits to maximize the rounding discrepancy?
validation_requirements: Test with 100+ users and partial fulfillment to measure divergence
Track sum(queuedDeposit[*][e]) vs depositEpochState[e].assetsDeposited across epochs
Verify the last user to execute gets fewer shares due to inflated epoch denominator

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 100 users each deposit 1001 assets. Manager fulfills 50%. Per-user fulfilled = mulDiv(1001, 500, 1001) = 500 (rounded). Per-user remainder = 501. Sum of remainders = 50100. But epoch-level rollover = 1001*100 - 500*100 = 50100. In this case it's exact, but with odd numbers like 3 users depositing 10 each and 50% fulfillment, discrepancies emerge. Over many epochs, this compounds.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited per-epoch (up to N-1 units dust), but compounds over time; last executor in each epoch bears disproportionate loss
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Last user(s) to call executeDeposit for partially-fulfilled epochs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Dust permanently orphaned; cannot be recovered

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing multi-user partial fulfillment rounding test

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by summing per-user queued amounts vs epoch-level totals

threats.title: Zero Fulfillment Amount Creates Permanently Unexecutable Epoch via Division by Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: If the manager calls fulfillDeposits(0, ...) or fulfillWithdrawals(0, ..., ...), the epoch state is written with assetsFulfilled=0/sharesFulfilled=0 and the epoch advances. Subsequent calls to executeDeposit or executeWithdrawal for that epoch will encounter division by zero in the mulDiv calculations (dividing by assetsFulfilled or sharesFulfilled), causing permanent reverts. All deposits/withdrawals queued in that epoch become permanently stuck.
threats.investigation_rationale: Neither fulfillDeposits nor fulfillWithdrawals validate that the fulfillment amount is greater than zero. The epoch counter is incremented regardless, making the broken epoch irreversible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No require(assetsToFulfill > 0) in fulfillDeposits; sharesReceived = mulDiv(0, totalSupply, totalAssets) = 0; stored as assetsFulfilled=0, sharesReceived=0

preconditions: Manager calls fulfillDeposits with assetsToFulfill=0 or fulfillWithdrawals with sharesToFulfill=0
Users have queued deposits/withdrawals in the epoch being fulfilled

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No input validation on fulfillment amount; epoch advances unconditionally
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: executeDeposit line 240: mulDiv(X, 0, 0) reverts; executeWithdrawal line 272: division by 0 in sharesFulfilled
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum fulfillment amount check; no way to re-process a broken epoch

analysis_questions: Does Math.mulDiv(0, 0, 0) revert or return 0?
Can the rolled-over amounts from a zero-fulfilled epoch be recovered?
Is there any mechanism for the manager to fix a broken epoch?
validation_requirements: Call fulfillDeposits(0, ...) and verify epoch advances with zero values
Call executeDeposit for that epoch and confirm revert
Verify assets are permanently stuck

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager accidentally calls fulfillDeposits(0, managerInput). Epoch increments, epoch state has assetsFulfilled=0, sharesReceived=0. All deposits roll to next epoch via aggregate rollover. But executeDeposit for the broken epoch reverts on mulDiv(X, 0, 0). Users with deposits in that epoch can never execute. Their per-user rollover never happens. Only cancelDeposit from the NEXT epoch could help, but the user's position in the broken epoch is inaccessible.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All deposits/withdrawals in the affected epoch become permanently stuck
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with queued deposits/withdrawals in the zero-fulfilled epoch
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No on-chain recovery mechanism for broken epoch; aggregate rollover preserves epoch-level amounts but per-user execution is blocked

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge case test for zero fulfillment amount

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring fulfillment events with 0 values

threats.title: Default feeWallet address(0) Blocks All Profitable Withdrawal Executions
threats.hypothesis_description: The feeWallet state variable defaults to address(0) since it's not set in the constructor. If performanceFeeBps > 0 and feeWallet is never set via setFeeWallet(), any executeWithdrawal where the user has profit (assetsToWithdraw > withdrawnBasis) will attempt SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer to address(0). Most ERC20 tokens revert on transfer to address(0), permanently blocking all profitable withdrawal executions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The constructor sets performanceFeeBps but does not set feeWallet. There is no deployment script evidence showing setFeeWallet is called. The executeWithdrawal path unconditionally transfers to feeWallet when performanceFee > 0.
deployment_assumptions: setFeeWallet() must be called before any profitable withdrawal execution
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence; foundry.toml does not reference deployment scripts. Constructor does not set feeWallet.

proof_of_signal.pattern: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(underlyingToken, feeWallet, performanceFee) where feeWallet defaults to address(0)

preconditions: performanceFeeBps > 0 (set in constructor)
feeWallet == address(0) (not set post-deployment)
User withdrawal has profit (assetsToWithdraw > withdrawnBasis)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Transfer to feeWallet without checking if feeWallet is set
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: feeWallet defaults to address(0) and is not validated before use
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(feeWallet != address(0)) in constructor or executeWithdrawal

analysis_questions: Does SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer revert on address(0) transfer for standard ERC20s?
Is there any deployment script that atomically sets feeWallet?
Can a user trigger this DoS by deliberately creating profitable withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Deploy vault with performanceFeeBps > 0 without calling setFeeWallet
Create profitable withdrawal and attempt executeWithdrawal
Confirm revert on transfer to address(0)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault is deployed with 10% performance fee but feeWallet is forgotten. Users deposit, vault earns profits, users request withdrawals. On executeWithdrawal, performanceFee is calculated as positive, triggering SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer to address(0). Transaction reverts. All profitable withdrawals are blocked until owner notices and sets feeWallet.
prerequisites: feeWallet not set before profitable withdrawals are executed

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All profitable withdrawal executions permanently blocked until feeWallet is set
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with profitable positions attempting withdrawal execution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Recoverable once owner calls setFeeWallet() with valid address

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for executeWithdrawal with unset feeWallet

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Immediate upon first profitable withdrawal attempt
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Instantly detectable as all profitable withdrawals revert

threats.title: NAV Underflow in fulfillDeposits/fulfillWithdrawals Permanently Bricks Epoch Advancement
threats.hypothesis_description: The totalAssets calculation in both fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals computes NAV + 1 - assetsDeposited - reservedWithdrawalAssets. If the vault's NAV drops below the sum of pending deposits and reserved withdrawal assets (due to investment losses or market conditions), this underflows in unchecked Solidity 0.8 and reverts. The manager cannot advance epochs, permanently blocking all deposit and withdrawal processing until NAV recovers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The vault allows the manager to invest deposited assets (including those pending fulfillment) via manage(). If these investments lose value, NAV can drop below the liabilities (pending deposits + reserved withdrawals). The protocol has no circuit breaker or alternative fulfillment path for this scenario.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalAssets = accountant.computeNAV(...) + 1 - epochState.assetsDeposited - reservedWithdrawalAssets; underflow if NAV < deposits + reserved - 1

preconditions: Manager invests pending deposit assets or reserved withdrawal assets via manage()
Investment losses cause NAV to drop below sum of pending deposits and reserved withdrawals

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unsigned subtraction without underflow protection on NAV-derived calculation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No fallback mechanism when vault is underwater; fulfillment is impossible
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that NAV >= deposits + reserved; no emergency fulfillment path

analysis_questions: Can the manager still call cancelDeposit to return funds when fulfillment is bricked?
How long could the vault remain bricked before NAV recovers?
Is there a governance mechanism to inject capital?
validation_requirements: Simulate vault with invested assets taking 50% loss
Verify fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals both revert
Check if cancelDeposit still works as escape hatch

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault has 1000 USDC in pending deposits and 500 USDC in reserved withdrawals. Manager invests all available capital. Market crash causes NAV to drop to 1200. totalAssets = 1200 + 1 - 1000 - 500 = underflow → revert. Neither deposits nor withdrawals can be fulfilled. Users' assets are locked until market recovers or manager injects capital.
prerequisites: Investment losses sufficient to cause NAV < pending deposits + reserved withdrawals

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete protocol halt; all pending deposits and fulfilled-but-unexecuted withdrawals locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May recover if NAV increases; cancelDeposit may work as partial escape

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for vault underwater scenario

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable when fulfillment transactions start reverting

threats.title: Unsafe uint128 Truncation in Fulfillment State Storage Causes Supply-Accounting Divergence
threats.hypothesis_description: In fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals, computed values (sharesReceived, assetsReceived, etc.) are cast to uint128 when stored in epoch state structs, but totalSupply is updated using the full uint256 value. If any computed value exceeds type(uint128).max, the stored epoch state is silently truncated while totalSupply reflects the true value. When users call executeDeposit, they receive shares based on the truncated value, creating permanently orphaned shares in totalSupply.
threats.investigation_rationale: The documentation states values should not exceed 2^126 - 1, but no on-chain enforcement exists. The unsafe cast uint128(sharesReceived) does not revert on overflow, creating a silent accounting divergence between totalSupply and distributable shares.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sharesReceived: uint128(sharesReceived) - unsafe downcast; totalSupply = _totalSupply + sharesReceived uses full uint256

preconditions: Computed sharesReceived or assetsReceived exceeds type(uint128).max
Requires very large deposit amounts relative to totalAssets or extreme share dilution

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unsafe uint128 cast without overflow check on fulfillment state values
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: totalSupply uses uint256(sharesReceived) but epoch state stores uint128(sharesReceived) - divergence on overflow
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No SafeCast or require statement to prevent truncation

analysis_questions: What token configurations would cause values to exceed uint128?
Is the 2^126 invariant enforced anywhere on-chain?
Can the manager manipulate totalAssets (via NAV) to create extreme share ratios?
validation_requirements: Calculate the minimum deposit amount that causes uint128 overflow given initial totalSupply=1_000_000
Verify that totalSupply diverges from sum of distributed shares on overflow
Test with edge-case token decimals (e.g., 30+ decimals)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If totalAssets = 1 (edge case on first fulfillment) and assetsToFulfill is large enough, sharesReceived = assetsToFulfill * 1_000_000 could exceed 2^128. Stored as truncated uint128, but totalSupply gets full value. Users can only claim shares based on truncated epoch state, permanently locking the excess in totalSupply.
prerequisites: Extremely large deposit amounts or extreme totalAssets ratios

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent share dilution; totalSupply inflated with unclaimable phantom shares
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All shareholders diluted by orphaned totalSupply inflation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot recover orphaned shares in totalSupply

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing overflow test for uint128 casts in fulfillment functions

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Requires deposit > ~3.4e38 smallest units to trigger
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing totalSupply with sum of epoch sharesReceived

threats.title: Epoch State Overwrite During Potential Reentrancy Through accountant.computeNAV
threats.hypothesis_description: In fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals, accountant.computeNAV() is called before the epoch state is finalized and epoch counter incremented. If IVaultAccountant interface does not declare computeNAV as 'view', the compiler uses CALL instead of STATICCALL, allowing a malicious or misconfigured accountant to reenter the vault. During reentrancy, calls like requestDeposit would modify depositEpochState[currentEpoch].assetsDeposited, but the outer fulfillDeposits overwrites this state using the pre-reentrancy snapshot, silently erasing the reentrant deposit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The IVaultAccountant interface source is not provided. If computeNAV is not declared as view, reentrancy through the accountant call is possible. The epoch state is read before the external call but written after, creating a classic read-before-write reentrancy pattern.
deployment_assumptions: IVaultAccountant.computeNAV must be declared as view for STATICCALL protection
threats.config_evidence: IVaultAccountant interface source not provided; cannot verify view declaration

proof_of_signal.pattern: accountant.computeNAV() external call at line 305-307 before epoch state write at lines 313-327; epochState read at line 302 before external call

preconditions: IVaultAccountant interface does not declare computeNAV as view
Accountant contract (set by owner) can make reentrant calls to the vault
Deposit or withdrawal requests are made during the reentrant call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State snapshot read before external call; state overwritten after; no reentrancy guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: depositEpochState[currentEpoch] read at L302, external call at L305, overwritten at L313 using pre-call snapshot
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier; reliance on STATICCALL requires interface verification

analysis_questions: Does IVaultAccountant declare computeNAV as view?
Can the owner set an accountant that reenters the vault?
What state mutations during reentrancy would be overwritten?
validation_requirements: Inspect IVaultAccountant interface for view modifier
Create malicious accountant that reenters requestDeposit during computeNAV
Verify the reentrantly-deposited assets are lost from accounting

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If computeNAV is not view, owner sets a malicious accountant. During fulfillDeposits, the accountant calls requestDeposit, adding assets to the current epoch. But fulfillDeposits then overwrites depositEpochState[currentEpoch] with the pre-call snapshot, erasing the reentrant deposit. The depositor's tokens are in the vault but their deposit is not tracked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Deposits made during reentrancy are lost from accounting; tokens trapped in vault
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user whose requestDeposit executes during the reentrancy window
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery mechanism for untracked deposits

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing reentrancy test through accountant callback

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring deposit events that don't match epoch state

threats.title: Double mulDiv Rounding in executeDeposit Systematically Shorts Users on Shares
threats.hypothesis_description: executeDeposit performs two consecutive mulDiv operations: first computing userAssetsDeposited (queuedAmount * assetsFulfilled / assetsDeposited), then sharesReceived (userAssetsDeposited * sharesReceived / assetsFulfilled). Each rounds down independently, compounding the rounding error. A single-step calculation mulDiv(queuedAmount, sharesReceived, assetsDeposited) would be more precise. The lost dust shares accumulate in totalSupply with no claimant.
threats.investigation_rationale: The two-step calculation introduces an intermediate rounding that the single-step equivalent would not have. With partial fulfillment, the intermediate value userAssetsDeposited is rounded, then used as input to a second rounding division. The error is at most 1 wei per step but applies to every user every epoch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Two consecutive mulDiv calls: userAssetsDeposited = mulDiv(A, B, C) then sharesReceived = mulDiv(userAssetsDeposited, D, B); intermediate rounding compounds

preconditions: Partial deposit fulfillment (assetsFulfilled < assetsDeposited)
Values that don't divide evenly in both mulDiv steps

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Consecutive mulDiv with intermediate rounding: mulDiv(mulDiv(X,A,B), C, A)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Could be simplified to single mulDiv(X, C, B) eliminating intermediate rounding
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No rounding direction documentation or dust accounting

analysis_questions: Quantify maximum per-user share loss from double rounding vs single step
Does the manager benefit from triggering repeated partial fulfillments to amplify rounding?
Over 1000 epochs with 100 users each, how much dust accumulates?
validation_requirements: Compare two-step vs single-step mulDiv for various input values
Measure cumulative dust over many epochs
Verify dust is permanently locked in totalSupply

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager repeatedly partially fulfills small amounts across many epochs. Each epoch, each user loses up to 2 wei of shares due to double rounding. Over 1000 epochs with 100 users, up to 200,000 wei of shares are permanently orphaned. While individual loss is tiny, the pattern is systematic and could be amplified by a malicious manager choosing fulfillment amounts that maximize rounding loss.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Dust-level per user per epoch; cumulative over protocol lifetime

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing precision comparison test between two-step and single-step calculation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by tracking totalSupply vs sum of all balanceOf

threats.title: Manager Can Drain Reserved Withdrawal Assets via Arbitrary manage() Calls
threats.hypothesis_description: The manage() function allows the manager to make arbitrary external calls from the vault, including ERC20 transfers of the underlying token. There is no check that the vault retains at least reservedWithdrawalAssets worth of underlying tokens after the call. A compromised manager can drain all assets including those reserved for already-fulfilled withdrawals, making executeWithdrawal permanently revert for all users.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the manager is trusted by design, a single compromised key can drain the entire vault including assets that users have already been promised (fulfilled withdrawals). The lack of any minimum balance enforcement creates a single point of failure.

proof_of_signal.pattern: target.functionCallWithValue(data, value) with no restriction on target or data; can transfer underlying token out

preconditions: Manager private key compromised or manager acts maliciously
Vault holds underlying tokens including reservedWithdrawalAssets

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unrestricted arbitrary call with no post-condition check on vault balance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum balance enforcement; no restriction on calling underlying token

analysis_questions: Could manage() be restricted to prevent underlying token transfers beyond a threshold?
Is there a way to enforce reservedWithdrawalAssets as a minimum balance?
What is the trust model for the manager key?
validation_requirements: Call manage to transfer all underlying tokens out
Verify executeWithdrawal reverts for all users afterward
Confirm users who burned shares for withdrawal have no recovery

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager key is compromised. Attacker calls manage(underlyingToken, abi.encodeWithSelector(IERC20.transfer.selector, attacker, vault.balance), 0). All underlying tokens are drained. Users with fulfilled but unexecuted withdrawals call executeWithdrawal which reverts on SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer due to insufficient balance. Their shares were already burned during requestWithdrawal, so they have no recourse.
prerequisites: Manager key compromise or malicious manager

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of all vault assets including reserved withdrawal assets
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vault users; worst for those with fulfilled but unexecuted withdrawals (shares already burned)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test verifying manage() cannot drain reserved assets

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: N/A - attacker gains all vault assets

threats.title: Withdrawal Epoch Rollover Rounding Creates Per-User/Epoch-Level Accounting Divergence
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeWithdrawal, sharesToFulfill is computed as (pendingAmount * sharesFulfilled / sharesWithdrawn), rounding down. The per-user sharesRemaining = amount - sharesToFulfill is thus rounded up. Similarly, withdrawnBasis uses the same rounding pattern. The aggregate rollover from fulfillWithdrawals uses exact subtraction at the epoch level, but per-user rollovers can sum to more than the epoch-level rollover. This mirrors the deposit rollover issue (TECH-H003) and causes the last withdrawal executor per epoch to receive fewer assets than their pro-rata share.
threats.investigation_rationale: Same rounding mismatch as deposits but in the withdrawal path. Each user's unfulfilled shares slightly exceed the epoch-level allocation due to rounding, causing the sum of per-user claims in the next epoch to exceed the epoch's total.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sharesToFulfill rounds down per user; sharesRemaining rounds up; sum of per-user rollovers exceeds epoch-level rollover from fulfillWithdrawals

preconditions: Partial withdrawal fulfillment with multiple users
Non-exact division in sharesToFulfill calculation

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-user rollover not synchronized with epoch-level rollover totals
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reconciliation mechanism between per-user and epoch-level withdrawal totals

analysis_questions: Does the discrepancy cause actual asset loss for the last executor?
Can the discrepancy cause an underflow in pro-rata calculations?
How does this interact with the basis rounding in the same function?
validation_requirements: Test with 10+ users and partial fulfillment
Sum all per-user rollovers and compare to epoch-level total
Verify last executor's pro-rata share is reduced

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 5 users each have 101 shares in withdrawal. 50 out of 505 total fulfilled. Each user's sharesToFulfill = 101*50/505 = 10 (rounded). sharesRemaining = 91 each. Sum = 455. But epoch-level rollover = 505-50 = 455. Exact here. With different numbers (e.g., 3 users at 100, 150 fulfilled out of 300): user1 = 100*150/300 = 50, remaining 50. All exact. Need to find a case where it diverges. With 3 users at 33: sharesToFulfill = 33*50/99 = 16 each. Remaining = 17 each. Sum = 51. Epoch rollover = 99-50=49. Divergence of 2!
prerequisites: Multiple users with partial fulfillment yielding non-exact division

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited per-epoch dust; compounds over protocol lifetime; last executor bears disproportionate loss
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Last withdrawal executor per partially-fulfilled epoch

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing multi-user partial withdrawal rollover rounding test

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by summing per-user withdrawal queues vs epoch-level state
